
# Name: Maxwell Braseth 
# Date: 06/14/2026
# Module 4 Homework

# ```{r}
install.packages("plotly")
install.packages("dplyr")
install.packages("ggplot2")
library(plotly)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
set.seed(42)

# 1.) 
# In your repo, create the HW4.qmd and add it to your book 
# (_quarto.yml).

# 2.) 

# Suppose X1 has 4 categories, 
# X2 has 6 categories, and 
# X3 has 3 categories.

# X1 ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4} --> 4 categories
# X2 ∈ {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} --> 6 categories
# X3 ∈ {1, 2, 3} --> 3 categories
# X1 * X2 * X3 = 4 * 6 * 3 = 72 cells

x1 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
x2 <- c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)
x3 <- c(1, 2, 3)

# How many cells does the largest possible model contain?
total_cells <- (length(x1) * length(x2) * length(x3)) # (x1 * x2 * x3)
print(paste("The maximum number of cells within the model is:", total_cells))
print(paste("This is because the largest possible model (the saturated model) requires a unique cell for every possible joint combination of the categories, calculated as 4 × 6 × 3 = 72 cells."))
print(paste("These 72 cells represent the complete classification of all three covariates, spanning from (1, 1, 1) through (4, 6, 3), allowing the model to capture all of the independently predicted values of the combined covariate cell values."))

# How many parameters does the smallest possible model require? 
parameters_for_smallest_model <- 1
print(paste("The minimum number of parameters within the model is:", parameters_for_smallest_model))
print(paste("This is because the smallest possible model (the intercept-only model) which ignores all covariate information and assigns the same prediction value ",
    "to every observation regardless of the combinations of the values of X1, X2, and X3."))
print(paste("At the same time, this model needs a intercept constant value that provides the same prediction value 
regardless of the combinations of the covariates values of x1, x2, and x3. This also helps estimate the mean of this smallest model."))

# If a training dataset of n=30 observations is drawn uniformly at 
# random from this covariate space, 
# roughly how many cells would you expect to be unobserved?

# Set parameters
set.seed(42)
n <- 30

#  Mathematical Formula for the Expected Number of Unobserved Cells
# E(cells_unobserved = UC) = total_cells = M * (1 - 1 / total_cells + M)^n
cells_unobserved_expected <- total_cells * (1 - 1 / total_cells)^n
print(paste("Theoretical expected unobserved cells:", round(cells_unobserved_expected, 0)))

# graph explanation to the observed and unobserved cells

# Continuous Uniform Simulation
# uniformly between 0 and 1 for n=30 observations for x, y, and z
idx_x1 <- sample(x1, n, replace = TRUE)
idx_x2 <- sample(x2, n, replace = TRUE)
idx_x3 <- sample(x3, n, replace = TRUE)
# creating the cell matrix/grid
grid_matrix <- expand.grid(
  x1 = x1,
  x2 = x2,
  x3 = x3
)
# Initialize observation count
grid_matrix$Obs_Count <- 0
# Looping throught the total n observations and filling the matrix/grid cells
for(i in 1:n){
  matched_row <- which(
    grid_matrix$x1 == idx_x1[i] &
    grid_matrix$x2 == idx_x2[i] &
    grid_matrix$x3 == idx_x3[i]
  )
  # incrementing the observation count of matched values the covariates of x1=4, x2=6, and x3=3
  grid_matrix$Obs_Count[matched_row] <-
    grid_matrix$Obs_Count[matched_row] + 1
}

# Number of unobserved cells and observed cells
simulated_unobserved <- sum(grid_matrix$Obs_Count == 0)
simulated_observed <- sum(grid_matrix$Obs_Count > 0)

print(paste("Simulated unobserved cells:", simulated_unobserved))
print(paste("Simulated observed cells:", simulated_observed))
print(paste("Difference between simulated and expected unobserved cells:",round(simulated_unobserved - cells_unobserved_expected, 0)))



# 3.) 

# Using the linear interpolation model, calculate the prediction ŷ(X0 = 0.45)
# Wa = (Xb - X0) / (Xb - Xa)
# Wb = (X0 - Xa) / (Xb - Xa)
# ŷ(X0) = Wa * ya + Wb * yb
# given training points and the observed value of x0: x0=0.45, xa = 0.38, ya = 1.92, xb = 0.57, yb = 2.04. 
x0 <- 0.45
ya <- 1.92
xa <- 0.38
xb <- 0.57
yb <- 2.04
# Show the weights Wa and Wb
wa <- (xb - x0) / (xb - xa)
wb <- (x0 - xa) / (xb - xa)
# setting the value of ŷ(X0)
prediction_y_x0 <- wa * ya + wb * yb
# verifying that the weights of Wa and Wb sum to 1
print(paste("The sum of the weights is:", wa + wb))
print(paste("Difference between the prediction value of ŷ(X0) and the total weights of wa & wb:", round(prediction_y_x0 - (wa + wb), 0)))

# what is the relative magnitudes of the weights, tell you about how much
# each neighbor contributes to the prediction.


# given that the neighbors are considered to be the two training observations that help show the prediction of ŷ(X0)
# and the neighbors are the left neighbor: wa=(Xa = 0.38, ya = 1.92) and the right neighbor: wb=(Xb = 0.57, yb = 2.04).
# At the same time, these neighbords are closets values to the observed value of x0 to the left and right of this observed value.

# relative magnitude(s) of the weights
print(paste("Relative magnitude ratio for wa/wb:", round(wa/wb), 2))
print(paste("Relative magnitude ratio for wb/wa:", round(wb/wa), 2))
print(paste("Relative magnitude of the left neighbor:", round(wa / (wa + wb), 2)))
print(paste("Relative magnitude of the right neighbor:", round(wb / (wa + wb), 2)))
print(paste("Difference between the relative magnitude of the left neighbor and the right neighbor:", round(wa / (wa + wb) - wb / (wa + wb), 0)))
print(paste("Is the left or the right neighbor larger or smaller?: ", "The left neighbor is larger than the right neighbor?: ", wa > wb, " The right neighbor is larger than the left neighbor?: ", wb > wa))
if(wa > wb){
    print(paste("The left neighbor is larger than the right neighbor and this means that the relative magnitude of the left neighbor contributes more to the prediction of ŷ(X0) at a wa value of : ", round(wa, 2) * 100, "%"))
    print(paste("This also means that the left neighbor wa is closer to the observed value of x0 than the right neighbor wb."))
    print(paste("Which also means that this linear interpolation model is better at predicting ŷ(X0) at a wa value of : ", round(wa, 2) * 100, "%"))
}else{
    print(paste("The right neighbor is larger than the left neighbor and this means that the relative magnitude of the right neighbor contributes more to the prediction of ŷ(X0) at a wb value of : ", round(wb, 2) * 100, "%"))
    print(paste("This also means that the right neighbor wb is closer to the observed value of x0 than the left neighbor wa."))
    print(paste("Which also means that this linear interpolation model is better at predicting ŷ(X0) at a wb value of : ", round(wb, 2) * 100, "%"))
}

# 4.) Using the following data, calculate the 3 nearest neigbhor prediction 
# for X0 = 0.2, 0.5, 0.7 by hand.

#   x    y
# 0.22 2.14
# 0.37 1.89
# 0.57 1.99
# 0.66 1.88
# 0.03 2.26
# 0.92 2.11
# 0.77 1.92
# 0.73 1.98
# 0.11 2.47
# 0.40 2.10

# Before selecting the 3 closest k=3 neighbors, measuring the distance between the new observed point (x) and a known training point (y) across (n) different features.
# Distance Metrics Formulas: 
# Euclidean Distance: Σ(√(xi-yi)^2) - yi : sqrt((x2 - x1)^2 + (y2 - y1)^2)
# Euclidean Distance in this context: Σ(x0-xi) - yi : |x2 - x1| 
# Manhatten Distance: Σ|xi - yi| - yi : |x2 - x1| + |y2 - y1|
# 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.2
# 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.5
# 3 nearest neighbors for x0 = 0.7


# where x0 = 0.2
# f(xi) = Σ(x0-xi)
# f(x=0.22) = 0.20 - 0.22 = |-0.02| = 0.02 --> y = 2.14
# f(x=0.37) = 0.20 - 0.37 = |-0.17| = 0.17 --> y = 1.89
# f(x=0.57) = 0.20 - 0.57 = |-0.37| = 0.37 --> y = 1.99
# f(x=0.66) = 0.20 - 0.66 = |-0.54| = 0.54 --> y = 1.88
# f(x=0.03) = 0.20 - 0.03 = |0.17| = 0.17 --> y = 2.26
# f(x=0.92) = 0.20 - 0.92 = |-0.72| = 0.72 --> y = 2.11
# f(x=0.77) = 0.20 - 0.77 = |-0.43| = 0.43 --> y = 1.92
# f(x=0.73) = 0.20 - 0.73 = |-0.46| = 0.46 --> y = 1.98
# f(x=0.11) = 0.20 - 0.11 = |0.09| = 0.09 --> y = 2.47
# f(x=0.40) = 0.20 - 0.40 = |-0.20| = 0.20 --> y = 2.10

# where x0 = 0.5
# f(xi) = Σ(x0-xi)
# f(x=0.22) = 0.50 - 0.22 = |0.28| = 0.28 --> y = 2.14
# f(x=0.37) = 0.50 - 0.37 = |0.13| = 0.13 --> y = 1.89
# f(x=0.57) = 0.50 - 0.57 = |-0.07| = 0.07 --> y = 1.99
# f(x=0.66) = 0.50 - 0.66 = |-0.19| = 0.19 --> y = 1.88
# f(x=0.03) = 0.50 - 0.03 = |0.47| = 0.47 --> y = 2.26
# f(x=0.92) = 0.50 - 0.92 = |-0.58| = 0.58 --> y = 2.11
# f(x=0.77) = 0.50 - 0.77 = |-0.23| = 0.23 --> y = 1.92
# f(x=0.73) = 0.50 - 0.73 = |-0.27| = 0.27 --> y = 1.98
# f(x=0.11) = 0.50 - 0.11 = |0.39| = 0.39 --> y = 2.47
# f(x=0.40) = 0.50 - 0.40 = |-0.10| = 0.10 --> y = 2.10

# where x0 = 0.7
# f(xi) = Σ(x0-xi)
# f(x=0.22) = 0.70 - 0.22 = |0.48| = 0.48 --> y = 2.14
# f(x=0.37) = 0.70 - 0.37 = |0.33| = 0.33 --> y = 1.89
# f(x=0.57) = 0.70 - 0.57 = |0.13| = 0.13 --> y = 1.99
# f(x=0.66) = 0.70 - 0.66 = |-0.02| = 0.02 --> y = 1.88
# f(x=0.03) = 0.70 - 0.03 = |0.67| = 0.67 --> y = 2.26
# f(x=0.92) = 0.70 - 0.92 = |-0.08| = 0.08 --> y = 2.11
# f(x=0.77) = 0.70 - 0.77 = |-0.03| = 0.03 --> y = 1.92
# f(x=0.73) = 0.70 - 0.73 = |-0.07| = 0.07 --> y = 1.98
# f(x=0.11) = 0.70 - 0.11 = |0.59| = 0.59 --> y = 2.47
# f(x=0.40) = 0.70 - 0.40 = |0.30| = 0.30 --> y = 2.10


# The top three closest neighbors for x0 = 0.2 are:
# y = 2.14 due to ŷ(x = 0.22)=0.02, y = 2.47 due to ŷ(x = 0.11)=0.09, y = 1.89 due to ŷ(x=0.37)=0.17
# 3 nearest neigbhor prediction for x0 = 0.2 for ŷ(x0 = 0.2) = (2.14 + 2.47 + 1.89) / 3 = 2.1667 = 2.17

# The top three closest neighbors for x0 = 0.5 are:
# ŷ(x=0.57)= 0.07 --> y = 1.99, ŷ(x = 0.37)=0.13 --> y = 1.89, ŷ(x = 0.40)=0.10 --> y = 2.10
# 3 nearest neigbhor prediction for x0 = 0.5 for ŷ(x0 = 0.5) = (1.99 + 1.89 + 2.10) / 3 = 1.99333 = 1.99

# The top three closest neighbors for x0 = 0.7 are:
# ŷ(x=0.66)= 0.02 --> y = 1.88, ŷ(x=0.77)=0.03 --> y = 1.92, ŷ(x=0.73)=0.07 --> y = 1.98
# 3 nearest neigbhor prediction for x0 = 0.7 for ŷ(x0 = 0.7) = (1.88 + 1.92 + 1.98) / 3 = 1.926667 = 1.93

# 5.) Generate a plot of the 3 nearest neigbhor prediction 
# function overlayed on top of the scatterplot of x and y.
# You may do this by hand or by code.

#   x    y
# 0.22 2.14
# 0.37 1.89
# 0.57 1.99
# 0.66 1.88
# 0.03 2.26
# 0.92 2.11
# 0.77 1.92
# 0.73 1.98
# 0.11 2.47
# 0.40 2.10


data_original <- data.frame(
  x = c(0.22, 0.37, 0.57, 0.66, 0.03, 0.92, 0.77, 0.73, 0.11, 0.40),
  y = c(2.14, 1.89, 1.99, 1.88, 2.26, 2.11, 1.92, 1.98, 2.47, 2.10)
)

x0_values <- c(0.2, 0.5, 0.7)

for (x0 in x0_values) {
  smallest_dist <- data_original %>% 
    mutate(dist = abs(x - x0)) %>%
    arrange(dist) %>%
    slice(1:3)
  
   predict_y_vals_from_x0 <- mean(smallest_dist$y)
  
  cat("For X0 =", x0, "\n")
  print(smallest_dist[, c("x", "y", "dist")])
  cat("Prediction ŷ =", round(predict_y_vals_from_x0, 3), "\n\n")
}

# Data from the table above
x_data_new <- seq(0, 1, length.out = n)

# 3-NN prediction function using the ORIGINAL data
knn3_pred <- function(x_new, original_data_1, k=3) {
  sapply(x_new, function(xi) {
    data <- original_data_1 %>% mutate(dist = abs(original_data_1$x - xi))
    mean((data %>% arrange(dist) %>% slice(1:k))$y)
  })
}

preds <- knn3_pred(x_data_new, data_original)

# R scatter plot
plot(data_original$x, data_original$y, 
     pch = 16, col = "steelblue", cex = 1.5,
     main = "3-Nearest Neighbor Prediction (k=3)",
     xlab = "X", 
     ylab = "Y",
     ylim = range(c(data_original$y, preds)))

# Add the 3-NN prediction line
lines(x_data_new, preds, col = "tomato", lwd = 2)

# Adding grid and legend
grid()
legend("topright", 
       legend = c("New X Data: Training Data", "3-NN Prediction (k=3) value from the original data"), 
       col = c("steelblue", "tomato"), 
       pch = c(16, NA), 
       lty = c(NA, 1), 
       lwd = c(NA, 2), 
       bty = "n")


#6.) In your own words, explain why the “largest model”
# — which assigns an independent estimate to every covariate 
# combination — can be a poor choice in practice even though it
# fits the training data perfectly. 
# Connect your answer to the concept of information sharing introduced
# in the chapter, and describe what characteristic of the data-generating 
# process would make the largest model a reasonable choice.


# When considering the topic of why the "Largest Model" independently predicts and estimates the outcome for each cell in the combinations of covariates, it is important to recognize that the model assigns a separate prediction to every possible combination of covariate values (for example, x1 = 4 categories, x2 = 6 categories, and x3 = 3 categories, resulting in 72 possible cells).
# The largest model can be a poor choice in practice because it simply tracks the mean outcome within each observed cell and does not share information across cells. While this allows the model to fit the training data perfectly, it does not provide a reliable way to make predictions for cells that are unobserved or contain very few observations. If some covariate combinations are not represented in the training data, then no cell-specific mean can be calculated for those combinations.
# This lack of information sharing can result in overfitting. The model may appear to perform extremely well on the observed training data because each cell is estimated independently, but its predictions may not generalize well to new data. In other words, the apparent error on the training data may be very small, while the prediction error on future observations may be much larger.
# When considering the concept of Information Sharing in this context, information sharing describes how observations contribute to predictions beyond their own cell. The best models often lie between the largest model (no information sharing) and the smallest model (maximum information sharing). These models allow observations to influence predictions in neighboring cells or groups, either locally (such as nearest-neighbor methods) or globally (such as regression models).
# Keeping this in mind, the largest model would be considered reasonable if the data-generating process truly produced independent outcomes for every covariate combination and there were sufficient observations within every cell to estimate the cell-specific mean accurately. In that situation, little or no information sharing would be needed because each cell could be reliably estimated using its own observed data.
# Finally, the largest model would be acceptable when all covariate combinations are well represented in the dataset and each cell contains enough observations to produce stable predictions. Under those conditions, the model could accurately describe the data-generating process without needing to borrow information from neighboring cells or groups.

# ```
